'''Kimi-Matias Räikkönen''' ({{IPA|fi|ˈkimi ˈmɑtiɑs ˈræi̯kːønen}}; born 17 October 1979) is a Finnish <!-- Please do not put "former" here; Räikkönen has retired from Formula One, he has not said he has retired from racing altogether -->[[racing driver|racing]] and [[rally driver]] who competed in [[Formula One]] between {{F1|2001}} and {{F1|2021}},{{efn|The exact years Räikkönen competed in [[Formula One]]: {{F1|2001}}–{{F1|2009}}, {{F1|2012}}–{{F1|2021}}.}} and the [[World Rally Championship]] from {{WRC|2009}} to {{WRC|2011}}. Nicknamed "'''the Iceman'''",{{efn|Räikkönen is nicknamed ''[[the Iceman (nickname)|the Iceman]]'' for his calm demeanour and Finnish nationality; he is also known for his reserved personality and reluctance to participate in [[public relations]] events.}} Räikkönen won the [[Formula One World Drivers' Championship]] in {{F1|2007}} with [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]], and won 21 [[Formula One Grands Prix|Grands Prix]] across 19 seasons.

Born in [[Espoo]], Räikkönen began competitive [[kart racing]] aged 10, winning several national titles before graduating to [[junior formulae]] in 1999. He dominated the [[2000 Formula Renault 2.0 UK Championship|2000 British Formula Renault Championship]] with [[Manor Motorsport|Manor]], prompting [[Sauber]] to sign him in Formula One for {{F1|2001}}. Following several points finishes across his rookie season, he joined [[McLaren]] in {{F1|2002}}, achieving his maiden podium [[2002 Australian Grand Prix|on debut]]. He achieved his maiden [[List of Formula One Grand Prix winners|victory]] at the {{F1GP|2003|Malaysian}} in {{F1|2003}}, as he finished runner-up to [[Michael Schumacher]]. Reliability issues with the [[McLaren MP4-19|MP4-19]] saw him drop to seventh the [[2004 Formula One World Championship|following year]], taking a sole victory in [[2004 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgium]]. He won seven Grands Prix as he finished runner-up to [[Fernando Alonso]] in {{F1|2005}}. After a winless {{F1|2006}} campaign, Räikkönen moved to [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]]; he took six victories as he beat Alonso and [[Lewis Hamilton]] to the {{F1|2007}} World Drivers' Championship by a single point. He finished third in {{F1|2008}}, with victories in [[2008 Malaysian Grand Prix|Malaysia]] and [[2008 Spanish Grand Prix|Spain]]. Replaced by Alonso for {{F1|2010}}, Räikkönen left Formula One at the end of his {{F1|2009}} campaign, where he won the {{F1GP|2009|Belgian}}.

Having debuted in [[rallying]] at the [[2009 Rally Finland]], Räikkönen signed with the [[Citroën Junior Team]] in the [[World Rally Championship]] for {{WRC|2010}}; he took a [[Special stage (rallying)|stage]] win at the [[2010 Rallye Deutschland|Rallye Deutschland]]. He continued in [[Citroën Racing|Citroën]] machinery as a [[privateer (motorsport)|privateer]] in {{WRC|2011}} under the [[ICE 1 Racing]] banner, finishing tenth overall in both seasons.<!-- He also made one-off [[NASCAR]] appearances in the [[2011 NASCAR Camping World Truck Series|Truck]] and [[2011 NASCAR Nationwide Series|Nationwide Series]].--> Räikkönen returned to Formula One with [[Lotus F1|Lotus]] in {{F1|2012}} and {{F1|2013}}, scoring their only victories at the [[2012 Abu Dhabi Grand Prix|2012 Abu Dhabi]] and [[2013 Australian Grand Prix|2013 Australian]] Grands Prix. He re-joined Ferrari for {{F1|2014}} to partner Alonso, who departed for [[Sebastian Vettel]] in {{F1|2015}}, when Räikkönen took several podiums as he finished fourth in the standings. Further podiums followed in {{F1|2016}} and {{F1|2017}}. In {{F1|2018}}—his last season with Ferrari—Räikkönen achieved his final career win at the {{F1GP|2018|United States}}, a record 114 races after his previous, and clinched third in the championship. He moved to [[Alfa Romeo]] in {{F1|2019}}, remaining with the team through {{F1|2020}} and {{F1|2021}} before retiring from Formula One.

Räikkönen achieved the third-most [[List of Formula One drivers who set a fastest lap|fastest laps]] (46), seventh-most [[List of Formula One driver records#Total podium finishes|podium finishes]] (103), and third-most [[List of Formula One driver records#Total starts|race starts]] (349)—all [[Formula One drivers from Finland|records for a Finnish driver]], alongside his 21 [[List of Formula One Grand Prix winners|victories]]—in Formula One history, where he still holds [[#Formula One records|several records]]<!--, including the [[List of Formula One driver records#Most fastest laps in a season|most fastest laps in a season]] (10, shared with [[Michael Schumacher]])-->. Since his retirement, he has made one-off appearances in the [[NASCAR Cup Series]] in [[2022 Go Bowling at The Glen|2022]] and [[2023 NASCAR Cup Series|2023]], and has served as team principal of [[Kawasaki Motors|Kawasaki]] in the [[Motocross World Championship]] since [[2022 FIM Motocross World Championship|2022]].

==Early life and career==
{{BLP sources section|date=August 2023}}

Kimi-Matias Räikkönen was born on 17 October 1979 in [[Espoo]], Finland.<ref name=IL>{{cite web|title=Kimi Räikkönen hauskuutti tietovisassa: "Hei, hei, tuo on veljeni!"|url=https://www.iltalehti.fi/formulat/a/2016060221666989|work=Iltalehti|access-date=4 February 2017|language=fi-FI|date=2 June 2016|quote=Croft julisti "Matias"-vastauksen oikeaksi, mutta Räikkönen kiiruhti korjaamaan – hänen etunimensä kun on oikeasti Kimi-Matias.|last=Stubin|first=Teemu|archive-date=12 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180712054318/https://www.iltalehti.fi/formulat/2016060221666989_fo.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref> He had success in [[Kart racing|karting]] from the age of ten. His first race outside Finland was in [[Monaco]] when he was 15 years old. During the race, the steering wheel broke, but he continued, informing his mechanic by frantically waving the steering wheel in the air on the home straight. Räikkönen's next Monaco race was also memorable; he was thrown on the wrong side of the safety fence in a collision but lifted his kart back onto the track and continued to race, finishing third.<ref>{{cite web | author=Kulta, Heikki | title=Hyvää kannatti odottaa kauan | work=[[Turun Sanomat]] | url=http://www.turunsanomat.fi/f1/?ts=1,3:2001:0:0,4:34:621:1:2007-10-22;4:35:0:0:0;4:36:0:0:0,104:34:493566,1:0:0:0:0:0: | access-date=21 October 2007 | language=fi-FI | archive-date=5 August 2013 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130805085740/http://ts.fi/moottoriurheilu/ | url-status=live }}</ref> In 1998 he was first in the Nordic Championship at Varna in Norway. In 1999, Räikkönen placed second in a round of the European Formula Super A championship for the Dutch PDB Racing Team. He also competed in the Formula Ford Euro Cup. By the age of twenty, he had won the [[British Formula Renault Championship|British Formula Renault]] winter series of 1999, winning the first four races of the year. In 2000, he won seven out of ten events in the [[British Formula Renault Championship|Formula Renault UK Championship]]. Combined, over these two series of Formula Renault, he won 13 out of 23 events – a 57% win rate.

==Formula One career==

===Sauber (2001)===
[[File:Sauber C20 front-right 2017 Hangar-7.jpg|thumb|right|[[Sauber C20]] of Räikkönen at [[Hangar-7]]]]
On the basis of these results, [[Peter Sauber]] gave the Finn a test with the [[Sauber]] Formula One team in September 2000 at the [[Mugello Circuit]].<ref name="Formula One magazine2">Tremayne, David. "Cool as Ice: Kimi Raikkonen". Formula One Magazine (official). December 2003. Page 63.</ref> On just the second day of the test, Räikkönen lapped half a second quicker than regular driver [[Pedro Diniz]]. Sauber had kept the news of his test quiet to distract potential competitors, internally referring to Räikkönen as "Eskimo".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Barretto |first1=Lawrence |title=Oral History: The inside story of Kimi Raikkonen's legendary first F1 test |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article.oral-history-the-inside-story-of-kimi-raikkonens-legendary-first-f1-test.3ModP4tC1WwNOAP8vjaLUy.html |website=Formula1.com |publisher=Formula One World Championship Limited |access-date=30 June 2020 |date=20 May 2019 |archive-date=2 June 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200602183914/https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article.oral-history-the-inside-story-of-kimi-raikkonens-legendary-first-f1-test.3ModP4tC1WwNOAP8vjaLUy.html |url-status=live }}</ref> After further tests in [[Circuito Permanente de Jerez|Jerez]] and [[Circuit de Catalunya|Barcelona]], Sauber signed Räikkönen for the {{F1|2001}} season. Although some critics (including [[Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile|FIA]] president [[Max Mosley]]) voiced concerns over granting an F1 [[FIA Super Licence|Super Licence]] to such an inexperienced driver, he was nevertheless granted his licence from the [[Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile]] (FIA) after a performance delivery promise by his team boss, Peter Sauber.<ref name="Formula1">{{cite web|url=http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/drivers/raikkonen.shtml|title=Formula 1 : Biography Kimi Raikkonen – F1-Live.com|access-date=22 October 2007|archive-date=20 October 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071020054040/http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/drivers/raikkonen.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref> He scored a championship point in his debut at the [[2001 Australian Grand Prix]].<ref name="Maurice Hamilton">{{cite news |author=Maurice Hamilton |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2008/mar/09/motorsports.formulaone2 |title=Hamilton, Maurice – "No fuss, just fast" |work=The Guardian |date=9 March 2008 |access-date=23 December 2010 |location=London |archive-date=15 December 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131215065846/http://www.theguardian.com/sport/2008/mar/09/motorsports.formulaone2 |url-status=live }}</ref>

Räikkönen had a solid debut year, achieving four points-scoring finishes and eight finishes in the top eight. Completing the year with nine points, he helped Sauber to what was then its best result of fourth place in the Constructors' Championship.

===McLaren (2002–2006)===
[[File:raikkonen.jpg|thumb|left|Räikkönen at the [[United States Grand Prix]] in 2002]]
Räikkönen, long linked to Sauber's engine supplier [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]], sufficiently impressed McLaren to earn a race seat in [[Ron Dennis]]'s team for {{F1|2002}}, taking the seat left vacant by double-[[list of Formula One World Drivers' Champions|world champion]], mentor and [[Formula One drivers from Finland|fellow Finn]] [[Mika Häkkinen]]. The decision to choose Räikkönen over his Sauber teammate, the Mercedes linked Nick Heidfeld, was heavily influenced by Häkkinen who repeatedly told Ron Dennis "If you wanna win, get the Finn."<ref>{{cite news |title=Todt: Michelin would have done the same. |url=https://www.carmag.co.za/news-blog/if-you-wanna-win-get-the-finn/ |access-date=13 January 2019 |work=carmag.co.za |date=27 November 2001 |archive-date=14 January 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190114100538/https://www.carmag.co.za/news-blog/if-you-wanna-win-get-the-finn/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

====2002====
Räikkönen scored a third-place podium finish in his first race with McLaren at the [[2002 Australian Grand Prix]]. Although McLaren suffered many engine failures in 2002, Räikkönen scored 24 points and four podiums, and held his own against teammate [[David Coulthard]]. Räikkönen came close to winning his first Grand Prix in [[2002 French Grand Prix|France]] but went off track at the Adelaide hairpin with a handful of laps to go, because of oil from the blown engine of [[Allan McNish]]'s [[Toyota F1|Toyota]] on the circuit.<ref name="Formula One magazine">Tremayne, David. "Cool as Ice: Kimi Raikkonen". Formula One Magazine (official). December 2003. Page 58.</ref> He finished the race second. He finished the season in sixth place, one place behind his teammate. Together they achieved a solid third place for McLaren in the Constructors' Championship.

====2003: Maiden win====
At the season-opening {{F1 GP|2003|Australian}}, Räikkönen qualified 15th in the spare car. In the race he took the lead before being caught speeding in the pitlane, caused by a software glitch in the car's electronic system. Räikkönen held off [[Michael Schumacher]] to finish third. In [[2003 Malaysian Grand Prix|Malaysia]], Räikkönen won his first race<ref name="Formula One magazine" /> after starting from seventh on the grid. During the next round in [[2003 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]], Räikkönen was declared the winner after the race was stopped on lap 55. According to the rules the winner is decided by the race order as of two laps before the race stopped, i.e. lap 53. However a week later, evidence emerged that [[Giancarlo Fisichella]] was on lap 56 when the race stopped, therefore the winner was decided by the order at lap 54. This granted the win to Fisichella, with Räikkönen second.

As other teams improved their cars, McLaren, who were still using the 2002 chassis, began to falter in terms of race speed.{{citation needed|date=August 2023}} However, Räikkönen finished second at [[2003 San Marino Grand Prix|Imola]]. At the {{F1 GP|2003|Spanish}}, Räikkönen made a mistake in qualifying and had to start from the back of the grid. At the start of the race, he collided with [[Antônio Pizzonia]], causing Räikkönen to retire from the race.

While having engine problems, Räikkönen successfully defended second position from [[Rubens Barrichello]] in [[2003 Austrian Grand Prix|Austria]]. He came extremely close to winning in [[2003 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]], but lost by less than a second to [[Juan Pablo Montoya]]. Starting from the pitlane in [[2003 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]] after he went off track during qualifying with understeer, Räikkönen finished sixth, more than a minute adrift of race winner Michael Schumacher.

[[File:Raikkonen 2003.jpg|thumb|left|Räikkönen at the [[2003 French Grand Prix]]]]
At the {{F1 GP|2003|European}}, Räikkönen took pole, and controlled the race from the start until his engine failed on lap 25. Title rival Michael Schumacher finished fifth taking 4 points advantage from Räikkönen. Räikkönen finished fourth in [[2003 French Grand Prix|France]] behind Schumacher, but finished one point ahead of him with a third-place finish at the {{F1 GP|2003|British}}. Räikkönen failed to finish the {{F1 GP|2003|German}} after being involved in an accident at the first corner with [[Ralf Schumacher]] and Rubens Barrichello. Räikkönen finished second at the next race, the {{F1 GP|2003|Hungarian}}.

Before the {{F1 GP|2003|Italian}}, the FIA were tipped-off by rivals Ferrari about an illegality in the [[Michelin]] tyre's tread width.<ref>{{cite news |title=Todt: Michelin would have done the same. |url=https://www.crash.net/f1/news/46939/1/todt-michelin-would-have-done-the-same |access-date=31 October 2018 |work=Crash.net |date=8 September 2003 |archive-date=31 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181031214923/https://www.crash.net/f1/news/46939/1/todt-michelin-would-have-done-the-same |url-status=live }}</ref> Michelin were forced to bring in narrower tyres. Räikkönen eventually finished fourth in the race, losing five championship points to race winner Michael Schumacher.

Räikkönen took pole at the {{F1 GP|2003|United States}}, but Michael Schumacher won the race with Räikkönen finishing second. After qualifying eighth in [[2003 Japanese Grand Prix|Japan]], Räikkönen finished second while Michael Schumacher just slipped into the points to win his sixth World Championship. Montoya's retirement during the race also meant that Räikkönen finished second in the championship, just two points behind Schumacher. The team also narrowly lost second place in the Constructors' Championship, finishing third, two points behind runners-up [[Williams Grand Prix Engineering|Williams]], and 12 points behind Ferrari.

====2004====
[[File:Mclaren racing usgp 2004.jpg|thumb|right|The McLaren mechanics push Räikkönen's MP4-19 into the garage during qualifying at the [[2004 United States Grand Prix]].]]
The {{F1|2004}} season began with Räikkönen only claiming a single point in the first seven races. His McLaren, especially the Mercedes engine, suffered repeated breakdowns, allowing him to complete just two of the first seven events. After seven rounds Räikkönen had only one point to Michael Schumacher's 60. In [[2004 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]], Räikkönen made five pit-stops but was classified fifth since the Williams-BMWs and the two Toyotas were disqualified. At the {{F1 GP|2004|United States}}, Räikkönen finished sixth.

At the {{F1 GP|2004|French}}, McLaren rolled out the new MP4-19B. Räikkönen finished seventh behind his team-mate, David Coulthard. At [[2004 British Grand Prix|Silverstone]], Räikkönen took pole and went on to finish second behind Michael Schumacher. The McLarens qualified on the second row of the grid in [[2004 German Grand Prix|Germany]]. Räikkönen lost his rear wing on lap 13 of the race while following race leader Michael Schumacher. He retired again from the {{F1 GP|2004|Hungarian}} after starting from 10th place on the grid, again on lap 13. At the {{F1 GP|2004|Belgian}}, Räikkönen qualified 10th, but took the lead on lap 11 and held onto it to take McLaren's only win of the season. He also took the fastest lap. The next weekend at [[2004 Italian Grand Prix|Monza]], Räikkönen again retired on lap 13, this time owing to electrical problems. At the next race in [[2004 Chinese Grand Prix|China]], he finished third, only 1.4 seconds behind race winner Rubens Barrichello.

At the {{F1 GP|2004|Japanese}}, Räikkönen was shunted by [[Felipe Massa]] on the first lap of the race, which caused him handling problems. He finished sixth, 2.5 seconds behind [[Fernando Alonso]]. At the last race of the season, the {{F1 GP|2004|Brazilian}}, he battled Montoya for the lead and finished one second behind him in second. Räikkönen ended the year seventh, with 45 points, only one behind sixth placed [[Jarno Trulli]], and four podiums.

Despite the disappointment of the 2004 season, Räikkönen was still seen as one of the rising stars of the sport. Many pundits predicted 2005 to be filled with great on-track battles from a resurgent team. He was also referred to by [[Ross Brawn]] and [[Jean Todt]] as a driver whom Ferrari might consider in the future. In early November 2004, Räikkönen announced his intention to create a racing team with his manager [[Steve Robertson (racing driver)|Steve Robertson]], to be entitled [[Double R Racing|Räikkönen Robertson Racing]] (otherwise known as "Double R"), which would compete in [[Formula Three]] in 2005.

====2005: Runner up to Alonso====
[[File:Lap4 Canada2005 Raikkonen.jpg|thumb|Räikkönen at the [[2005 Canadian Grand Prix]]]]
[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2005 USA.jpg|thumb|Räikkönen at the [[2005 United States Grand Prix]]]]
Räikkönen's start to the {{F1|2005}} season was less than perfect. The car was reported to be too soft on its Michelin tyres, with the result that it was not generating enough heat to post competitive qualifying times.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.autosport.com/motorsport/feature/328/the-2005-teams-review|title=The 2005 Teams Review – – Autosport Plus|first=Tom|last=Keeble|website=Autosport.com|date=26 October 2005 |access-date=9 September 2019|archive-date=12 August 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190812151707/https://www.autosport.com/motorsport/feature/328/the-2005-teams-review|url-status=live}}</ref> The best qualifying position that a McLaren driver could manage in the first three races was sixth. Räikkönen compounded this by stalling on the grid of the first race of the season, the {{F1 GP|2005|Australian}}, and ending the race with just a point. He looked set for a podium in [[2005 Malaysian Grand Prix|Malaysia]] until a faulty tyre valve failed and dropped him out of the points. [[2005 Bahrain Grand Prix|Bahrain]] saw him achieve his first podium of the season with a third place behind Renault's Alonso and the Toyota of [[Jarno Trulli]].

Räikkönen then achieved three consecutive poles in [[2005 San Marino Grand Prix|San Marino]], [[2005 Spanish Grand Prix|Spain]], and a win after a [[safety car]] strategy call by [[Neil Martin (motorsport)|Neil Martin]] at [[2005 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]]. An almost certain win was denied at Imola after a driveshaft failure, but he won the other two races, putting him within 22 points of leader Alonso. He registered strong, comfortable wins at Barcelona, beating Alonso, and at Monte Carlo, never dropping his lead in both races. At the {{F1 GP|2005|European}}, Räikkönen flat-spotted his right front tyre, causing his suspension to fail while he led on the final lap, handing a further ten points to his rival Alonso. This incident, in part, resulted in a rules clarification allowing teams to change a flat-spotted tyre without punishment.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.pitpass.com/public/article.php?fes_art_id=25355|title=FIA clarifies tyre ruling |website=pitpass.com|date=27 July 2005|access-date=9 September 2019|archive-date=16 December 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181216120627/https://www.pitpass.com/public/article.php?fes_art_id=25355|url-status=live}}</ref>

Alonso's first major mistake of the 2005 season handed the {{F1 GP|2005|Canadian}} to Räikkönen. The following weekend saw all the Michelin teams, including McLaren, withdraw from the {{F1 GP|2005|United States}} for safety reasons. At the {{F1 GP|2005|French}}, Räikkönen suffered a ten-place grid-penalty following the replacement of his new specification Mercedes Benz engine which failed in Friday practice. Räikkönen, putting in what Ron Dennis called his best ever qualifying lap,<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/main.jhtml?xml=/sport/2005/07/04/smfrgp04.xml | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071011211421/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/main.jhtml?xml=%2Fsport%2F2005%2F07%2F04%2Fsmfrgp04.xml | url-status=dead | archive-date=11 October 2007 | title=Telegraph – Alonso puts smile on French faces | work=The Daily Telegraph | location=London | first=Kevin | last=Garside | date=4 July 2005 | access-date=7 May 2010 }}</ref> qualified third (demoted to 13th) with a significant fuel load. He finished second behind Alonso. A week later at the {{F1 GP|2005|British}}, Räikkönen suffered another Mercedes engine failure due to an oil leak; his second-place qualifying place became 12th. He claimed third place in the race.

In [[2005 German Grand Prix|Germany]], Räikkönen was comfortably in the lead having dominated all weekend, but suffered a hydraulics failure, handing victory and a further 10 points to Alonso. It was his third retirement while leading a race during the season. At the opening of the {{F1 GP|2005|Hungarian}}, though saying he was very comfortable at McLaren, Räikkönen raised the possibility that he might leave McLaren when his contract expired in 2006, if reliability issues were not solved.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.itv-f1.com/News_Article.aspx?PO_ID=33631 |title=ITV – Kimi: Don't take me for granted |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070313003241/http://www.itv-f1.com/News_Article.aspx?PO_ID=33631 |archive-date=13 March 2007}}</ref> He went on to take the chequered flag with a convincing victory over Michael Schumacher, albeit after McLaren teammate [[Juan Pablo Montoya|Montoya]] retired.

Räikkönen won the Hungarian Grand Prix from the most handicapped qualifying position, having had to do his qualifying run first on the notoriously dusty and dirty track because of his early retirement a week earlier at Hockenheim. No other driver had previously managed this feat. Räikkönen then became the first ever winner of the {{F1 GP|2005|Turkish}}. Two weeks later at the {{F1 GP|2005|Italian}}, Räikkönen's pole position was taken from him as he received another 10-position grid penalty for an engine change. He eventually finished fourth.

Räikkönen went on to win for the second year in a row in [[2005 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgium]] at [[Spa-Francorchamps]]. The following race, the {{F1 GP|2005|Brazilian}}, saw Alonso clinch the Drivers' Championship after finishing third behind Montoya and Räikkönen. In the [[2005 Japanese Grand Prix|penultimate race of the year]], at the [[Suzuka Circuit]] in Japan, Räikkönen took his seventh victory of the season after starting 17th on the grid. The win was secured when he overtook Renault driver Fisichella (who had started third on the grid, and had led most of the race) on the final lap – which Formula One journalist [[Peter Windsor]] thought the most impressive move of the race.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.itv-f1.com/Feature.aspx?Type=Windsors_Wisdom&PO_ID=34324 |title=ITV – Peter Windsor's Chinese GP Preview |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061208085916/http://www.itv-f1.com/Feature.aspx?Type=Windsors_Wisdom&PO_ID=34324 |archive-date= 8 December 2006}}</ref>

Räikkönen received the ''[[F1 Racing]]'' "Driver of the Year" accolade,<ref name="F1 Racing Award">{{cite web| title = Awards of F1 Racing magazine| publisher = McLaren| date = 23 November 2005| url = http://www.kimicentral.net/content/view/19/30/| access-date = 28 March 2007| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070928215542/http://www.kimicentral.net/content/view/19/30/| archive-date = 28 September 2007| url-status = dead}}</ref> and the ''[[Autosport]]'' "International Racing Driver of the Year" award.<ref name="Autosport Award">{{cite news|title=Räikkönen pips Alonso to British award |agency=Reuters |date=6 December 2005 |url=http://tvnz.co.nz/view/page/411317/636352 |access-date=28 March 2007 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070926225041/http://tvnz.co.nz/view/page/411317/636352 |archive-date=26 September 2007}}</ref>

====2006: Final season at McLaren====
[[File:Kimmi raikkonen cheste 2006-02.jpg|left|thumb|Räikkönen testing for McLaren at [[Circuit de Valencia|Valencia]] in early 2006]]
In [[2006 Bahrain Grand Prix|Bahrain]], starting from 22nd place on the grid, he drove through the field, ending third behind Alonso and Michael Schumacher. In [[2005 Malaysian Grand Prix|Malaysia]], Räikkönen was hit from behind by [[Red Bull Racing]]'s [[Christian Klien]] on the first lap and retired.

Having started the year clearly behind Renault, McLaren improved in [[2006 Australian Grand Prix|Australia]], where Räikkönen finished second after flat spotting a tyre and losing a wing end-plate. Chasing down Alonso during the final stages of the race, he set the fastest lap of the race on the final lap, finishing only 1.8 seconds behind the Spaniard. At the {{F1 GP|2006|San Marino}}, a bad choice of strategy and a mistake from Räikkönen in qualifying saw the McLarens get caught in traffic in the early part of the race allowing Michael Schumacher and Alonso to get away at the front. Räikkönen eventually finished fifth, with teammate Montoya ahead in third place. McLaren team boss Ron Dennis blamed what he deemed to be Räikkönen's poor performance for the team's failure to finish in the top two in the race.<ref>{{cite news| title =McLaren downplay Ferrari victory| publisher =BBC| url =https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/4937146.stm| date =24 April 2006| access-date =1 June 2007| archive-date =27 April 2006| archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20060427122737/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/motorsport/formula_one/4937146.stm| url-status =live}}</ref>

[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2006 test.jpg|right|thumb|Räikkönen testing for McLaren at [[Silverstone Circuit|Silverstone]] in April 2006]]
At the {{F1 GP|2006|Spanish}}, Räikkönen qualified ninth. However, he managed to get up to fifth place on the first lap of the race. He retained this position for most of the race, finishing in fifth place. A few days after the Spanish Grand Prix, he admitted that he had no chance of winning the 2006 Championship.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.sportinglife.com/formula1/news/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=formula1/06/05/19/manual_140547.html|title=Sporting Lige – Schumacher: Title Race Open|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930043310/http://www.sportinglife.com/formula1/news/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=formula1%2F06%2F05%2F19%2Fmanual_140547.html|archive-date=30 September 2007}}</ref> In [[2006 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]], Räikkönen qualified third. During the race he got up to second and kept pace with Alonso, however he retired during a safety car period after a failed heat shield led to a wiring loom inside the car catching fire. After his retirement from this race, he was seen on live TV walking along the Monaco sidewalks still wearing his helmet, before going straight to the harbour instead of the pits and climbing aboard a yacht.

The {{F1 GP|2006|British}} at Silverstone saw Räikkönen qualify second behind Alonso and in front of Michael Schumacher. The running order was Alonso, Räikkönen, Schumacher until the second set of pitstops where Räikkönen was demoted to third by Schumacher, a position he held until the end of the race. In [[2006 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]], Räikkönen achieved another podium. In the {{F1 GP|2006|United States}}, his teammate punted him out in an expensive seven car accident. The {{F1 GP|2006|French}} saw Räikkönen qualify his car in sixth. His teammate was now former test driver [[Pedro de la Rosa]] in place of Montoya. Räikkönen ended the race in fifth. In [[2006 German Grand Prix|Germany]], Räikkönen qualified on pole. After a battle with [[Jenson Button]], he finished the race for the first time in his career, ending in third place. Another pole came in [[2006 Hungarian Grand Prix|Hungary]], but he collided with [[Vitantonio Liuzzi]] after 25 laps, causing his fourth retirement of the season.

A first turn incident with [[Scott Speed]] at the {{F1 GP|2006|Turkish}} led to an exploded tyre and suspension damage. After a tyre change, Räikkönen's race ended halfway into the next lap when he crashed into the barrier at turn 4 because of a loss of rear grip. Räikkönen qualified on pole for the {{F1 GP|2006|Italian}} by two thousandths of a second from Michael Schumacher. He led the early part of the race until the first pitstops where he was passed by Schumacher. He stayed in second place for the rest of the race. After the race, Schumacher announced that he would retire at the end of the season. Later, Ferrari announced that he would be replaced in the 2007 season by Räikkönen.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.formula1.com/news/headlines/2006/9/4933.html |title=Formula1.com – Ferrari confirm Raikkonen, Massa for '07 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071012234558/http://www.formula1.com/news/headlines/2006/9/4933.html |archive-date=12 October 2007}}</ref>

The {{F1 GP|2006|Chinese}} saw another retirement for Räikkönen due to throttle problems. His last two Grands Prix, in [[2006 Japanese Grand Prix|Japan]] and [[2006 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]], did lead to two finishes, but he missed the podium on both occasions. Räikkönen ended his time at McLaren-Mercedes with a fifth place in the World Drivers' Championship, with McLaren placing third in the World Constructors' Championship at the end of a winless year.

Räikkönen's [[British Formula 3 Championship]] team [[Double R Racing|Räikkönen Robertson Racing]] claimed their first major success, with British driver [[Mike Conway]] winning the [[2006 British Formula 3 season|2006 British F3 International Series]] title and the prestigious [[Macau Grand Prix]].

===Ferrari (2007–2009)===
After the [[2006 Italian Grand Prix]], Ferrari announced that Räikkönen had signed a three-year contract with Scuderia Ferrari for the 2007–2009 seasons. Räikkönen said after the move that he was very happy with this change of events but wished McLaren the best of luck in the future. He became the teammate to Brazilian [[Felipe Massa]], who had been driving for Ferrari since 2006.<!--Following the retirement of Michael Schumacher and his new deal with Ferrari, Räikkönen was estimated to be the world's highest-salaried star in any team sport, with a base salary reportedly worth $51 million annually. Other individuals may have made more from prize money, such as golfers, but no-one earned a higher base salary. NEEDS A RELIABLE SOURCE!-->

====2007: World Drivers' Champion====
[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2007 USA 2.jpg|thumb|Räikkönen driving for [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]] at the [[2007 United States Grand Prix]], where he finished fourth]]
[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2007 Britain.jpg|thumb|Räikkönen won his third race of {{F1|2007}} at [[2007 British Grand Prix|Silverstone]].]]
Räikkönen started the season in [[2007 Australian Grand Prix|Australia]] by taking pole position, setting the fastest lap, and becoming the first driver since [[Nigel Mansell]] in {{F1|1989}} to win his first Grand Prix with Ferrari.

At the {{F1 GP|2007|Malaysian}}, Räikkönen was passed by [[Lewis Hamilton]] at the start and remained behind him for the rest of the race, finishing third. In [[2007 Bahrain Grand Prix|Bahrain]], Räikkönen started from third but was passed by McLaren driver Fernando Alonso. He eventually regained third position from Alonso and finished the race third. At the {{F1 GP|2007|Spanish}}, Räikkönen retired after only 10 laps with an electrical problem. This took him down to fourth position in the Championship, behind team-mate Felipe Massa. At the {{F1 GP|2007|Monaco}}, Räikkönen struck a barrier in qualifying and broke his right front suspension. He started 16th and finished eighth.

In [[2007 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]], Räikkönen qualified fourth and finished fifth, Räikkönen's team-mate Massa was disqualified.<ref>{{cite news| url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/6739373.stm| title = Canadian Grand Prix| date = 8 July 2007| access-date = 19 July 2007| author = Benson, Andrew| publisher = BBC| archive-date = 4 July 2007| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070704223216/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/6739373.stm| url-status = live}}</ref> At the {{F1 GP|2007|United States}}, Räikkönen qualified fourth, finished fourth and recorded fastest lap of the race. With ten races in the season left, Räikkönen was 26 points behind leader Lewis Hamilton in the Drivers' Championship.

In [[2007 French Grand Prix|France]], Räikkönen qualified third, but overtook Hamilton at the first corner of the race. He subsequently ran second, behind teammate Massa, for much of the Grand Prix, but overtook the Brazilian during the pit stops and took his second victory of the season. This was the 11th victory of his Formula One career and Ferrari's first 1–2 win of the 2007 season.<ref>{{cite news| url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/6258728.stm| title = French GP| date = 10 June 2007| access-date = 19 July 2007| author = Croft, David| publisher = BBC| archive-date = 11 July 2007| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070711171623/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/6258728.stm| url-status = live}}</ref> At the {{F1 GP|2007|British}}, Räikkönen qualified in second place, just missing the pole by running wide in the last corner. In the race, he again took the lead through pit stops, first overtaking Lewis Hamilton midway through the race and then putting in fast laps as Alonso pitted for the second time in the closing stages to pass him. Räikkönen led to the end of the race.<ref>{{cite news| url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/6282190.stm| title = British Grand Prix 2007| date = 1 July 2007| access-date = 19 July 2007| author = Benson, Andrew| publisher = BBC| archive-date = 17 August 2007| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070817033929/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/6282190.stm| url-status = live}}</ref>

At the {{F1 GP|2007|European}}, Räikkönen captured his second pole position of the season but retired from the race, run in heavy rain, with a problem with the hydraulics of the car. In [[2007 Hungarian Grand Prix|Hungary]], Räikkönen qualified his car in fourth place, but started from third after Alonso was penalised. In the race he overtook [[Nick Heidfeld]] at the start and pressured Hamilton until the end, but had to settle for second, being 0.7s behind Hamilton. He set the fastest lap time on the last lap of the race, commenting after the race: "I was so bored behind Hamilton, I wanted to see how quick I could have been". In [[2007 Turkish Grand Prix|Turkey]], Räikkönen missed pole position after making a mistake in the final sector of his fast lap, which left him third on the grid. On race day, he overtook Hamilton in the first corner and took second place, which he kept to the end of the race.

At [[2007 Italian Grand Prix|Monza]]'s third practice session, Räikkönen crashed into the tyre wall before entering the Ascari chicane. He qualified in fifth place, and raced in the Ferrari reserve car while suffering from a neck problem. The Ferrari team employed an unusual one-stop strategy, which left him third after Hamilton passed him late in the race on fresh tyres.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.homeofsport.com/f1/news/item.aspx?id=20113|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304095518/http://www.homeofsport.com/f1/news/item.aspx?id=20113|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 March 2016|title=Home of Sport: Formula One: News: Räikkönen to race with stiff, sore neck}}</ref> At [[2007 Belgian Grand Prix|Spa-Francorchamps]], Räikkönen's favourite circuit,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.formula1.com/news/headlines/2007/9/6796.html|title=Belgian Grand Prix – selected driver quotes|website=Formula1.com|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071129193835/http://www.formula1.com/news/headlines/2007/9/6796.html|archive-date=29 November 2007|access-date=18 May 2020}}</ref> he secured pole position again and took his fourth victory of the season. Massa finished second, Alonso third and Hamilton fourth. This was also Räikkönen's third consecutive Spa win, which placed him among six other drivers with three or more Spa wins.

[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2007 Belgium.jpg|thumb|right|Räikkönen at [[2007 Belgian Grand Prix|Spa]], where he won his fourth race of the year]]
At the [[Fuji Speedway]] in [[2007 Japanese Grand Prix|Japan]], the only new track on the 2007 calendar, Räikkönen qualified in third position, while Hamilton took pole and Alonso second. In an extremely wet race, which saw the first 19 laps run behind the [[safety car]], both Räikkönen and teammate Massa were badly affected by having to change to extreme wet tyres during the early stages, because the FIA's tyre-rule notification arrived late at Ferrari.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.grandprix.com/race/r783sunquotes.html|title=Japanese GP – Team Quotes|access-date=12 September 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130809005855/http://www.grandprix.com/race/r783sunquotes.html|archive-date=9 August 2013|url-status=dead}}</ref> Towards the end of the race, Räikkönen moved through the field to third place, but could not pass his fellow countryman [[Heikki Kovalainen]] for second.

At the {{F1 GP|2007|Chinese}} in [[Shanghai International Circuit|Shanghai]], Räikkönen dominated the whole weekend with fastest laps in the free-practice sessions. In qualifying, Hamilton took pole position with a lighter fuel load, while Räikkönen qualified second and Massa third. There was light rainfall at the beginning of the race which prompted the cars to start on intermediate tyres. After the first round of pit stops Hamilton lost grip as his tyres suffered graining, and Räikkönen overtook him. Hamilton retired after sliding into a gravel trap in the pit lane. Räikkönen took his fifth win of the season, that revived his title hopes before the last race of the season. This was also the 200th race win and 600th podium in a World Championship event for Ferrari ''as a manufacturer'' (''as a team'', their 200th win was achieved at [[2007 Brazilian Grand Prix|the next race in Brazil]], and their 600th podium was achieved in [[2008 Bahrain Grand Prix]]). Räikkönen moved to seven and three points behind Hamilton and Alonso in the Drivers' Championship, respectively, going into the last race in Brazil, the first three-way title battle in the final race of the season since {{F1|1986}}.

[[File:Kimi Raikkonen won 2007 Brazil GP.jpg|left|thumb|Räikkönen celebrating victory and the world title at the [[2007 Brazilian Grand Prix]]]]
Räikkönen took the 2007 Formula One Drivers' title with victory in the {{F1 GP|2007|Brazilian}} at [[Autódromo José Carlos Pace|Interlagos]], in an incident-packed race. Massa had taken pole, followed by Hamilton, Räikkönen, and Alonso. At the start of the race Räikkönen passed Hamilton on the outside and lined up behind Massa. Alonso shortly afterwards passed Hamilton, who fell progressively down the order. Räikkönen eventually overtook Massa, who was already eliminated from contention for the Drivers' Championship in the Japanese Grand Prix. Massa's strategy for the second round of pit stops ensured Räikkönen kept the lead. Räikkönen went on to take the [[Racing flag#The chequered flag|chequered flag]], which handed him the crown by a single point from Hamilton and Alonso. Championship leader Hamilton eventually finished the race in seventh place, while defending champion Alonso managed third.

While Räikkönen had only one point more than Alonso and Hamilton at the end of the season, he had the most victories (six compared to four by each McLaren driver).<ref>{{cite news|title=Raikkonen the playboy king|publisher=BBC Sport|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7055633.stm|date=21 October 2007|access-date=22 November 2007|first=Andrew|last=Benson|archive-date=23 October 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071023063510/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7055633.stm|url-status=live}}</ref>

Räikkönen's Drivers' Championship was briefly put into doubt when race stewards began an investigation after identifying possible fuel irregularities in the cars of [[Nico Rosberg]], [[Robert Kubica]] and [[Nick Heidfeld]] following post-race inspection. Their disqualification and a race reclassification would have seen Hamilton lifted from seventh to fourth in the race result.<ref>{{cite news |url= https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7055644.stm |title= Inquiry casts doubt on F1 title |publisher= BBC Sport |date= 21 October 2007 |access-date= 21 October 2007 |archive-date= 23 October 2007 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20071023062729/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7055644.stm |url-status= live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url= http://www.odmotoring.com/index.php?display=racing&id=514 |title= Investigation throws title into doubt |publisher= OverDrive |date= 21 October 2007 |access-date= 21 October 2007 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20071025033239/http://www.odmotoring.com/index.php?display=racing&id=514 |archive-date= 25 October 2007 |url-status= dead }}</ref> However the race stewards decided that no sanctions would be given, meaning the results would stand.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.express.co.uk/sport/f1-autosport/28216/Raikkonen-title-confirmed|title=Raikkonen title confirmed|website=www.express.co.uk|date=21 October 2007|access-date=18 May 2020|archive-date=2 September 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210902130802/https://www.express.co.uk/sport/f1-autosport/28216/Raikkonen-title-confirmed|url-status=live}}</ref> McLaren appealed the decision,<ref name="McLaren appeal">{{cite news| title = F1 teams escape fuel punishment| url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7055644.stm| publisher = BBC| date = 22 October 2007| access-date = 22 October 2007| quote = McLaren has said it plans to appeal to the FIA, the sport's governing body.| archive-date = 23 October 2007| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20071023062558/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7055644.stm| url-status = live}}</ref> however the FIA Court of Appeal rejected their appeal on 16 November 2007 thus confirming Räikkönen as the champion.<ref>{{cite news |url= https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7099263.stm |title= McLaren lose Brazilian GP appeal |publisher= BBC |date= 16 November 2007 |access-date= 5 January 2010 |archive-date= 2 December 2010 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20101202082109/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7099263.stm |url-status= live }}</ref> As of the end of the [[2025 Formula One season]], Räikkönen's 2007 title triumph remains the most recent World Drivers' Championship win for a Scuderia Ferrari driver.

In January 2008, Räikkönen was beaten by [[Tero Pitkämäki]] in the race for the annually chosen [[Finnish Sports Personality of the Year]] award; finishing in [[Runner-up|second place]] in the competition.<ref>{{cite web | title=F1 champion Kimi Räikkönen beaten by Pitkämäki in race for annual sports award | work=[[International Association of Athletics Federations]] | url=http://www.iaaf.org/news/news/f1-champion-kimi-raikkonen-beaten-by-pitkamak | date=19 December 2007 | access-date=18 December 2015 | archive-date=1 April 2016 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160401202741/http://www.iaaf.org/news/news/f1-champion-kimi-raikkonen-beaten-by-pitkamak | url-status=live }}</ref>

====2008====
After a disappointing first race in [[2008 Australian Grand Prix|Australia]], where Räikkönen eventually finished eighth after qualifying only 15th due to a mechanical problem, he won his first race of the {{F1|2008}} season at the {{F1 GP|2008|Malaysian}}, finishing ahead of [[Robert Kubica]] and [[Heikki Kovalainen]]. His victory in Kuala Lumpur came on the fifth anniversary of his maiden victory at the same track. In [[2008 Bahrain Grand Prix|Bahrain]], Räikkönen qualified in fourth on the grid. He moved up to second place by the third lap and finished in that position, behind his team-mate [[Felipe Massa]]. He also secured the lead in the championship.

[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2008 Malaysia 3.jpg|thumb|left|Räikkönen took his first win of {{F1|2008}} at the {{F1 GP|2008|Malaysian}}.]]
In [[2008 Spanish Grand Prix|Spain]], Räikkönen took the 15th pole of his career and his first of the 2008 season. He managed to take his second race win of the season and the fastest lap of the race. Räikkönen overtook [[Mika Häkkinen]] in the list of total number of fastest laps and also in terms of podium finishes, making him the highest-ranked Finnish driver in these statistics.

At the {{F1 GP|2008|Turkish}}, Räikkönen qualified in fourth place. Despite damaging his front wing in the early stages after a collision with fellow Finn Heikki Kovalainen, Räikkönen was still able to set the fastest lap and finish in third place.

In [[2008 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]], Räikkönen qualified in second behind teammate Felipe Massa. Räikkönen stayed second behind Massa until he was given a drive-through penalty for an infringement by the team on his car and dropped down to sixth. He was set for fifth until an incident with [[Adrian Sutil]], when Räikkönen lost control on the damp track after exiting the tunnel, and hit Sutil's car in the rear. Räikkönen's car was not badly damaged and he was able to finish in ninth after replacing his front wing, also setting the fastest lap in the process.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7419319.stm|title=Hamilton wins Monaco after crash|publisher=BBC Sport|date=25 May 2008|access-date=5 January 2010|first=Andrew|last=Benson|archive-date=28 May 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080528040741/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7419319.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> After the race, [[Mike Gascoyne]], the Chief Technology Officer of Force India announced they were filing official protests with the stewards over the incident, demanding a ban for Räikkönen.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.planet-f1.com/story/0,18954,3213_3609378,00.html |title=Force India want Raikkonen banned |publisher=Planet F1 |date=25 May 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080526055328/http://www.planet-f1.com/story/0%2C18954%2C3213_3609378%2C00.html |archive-date=26 May 2008}}</ref> However, the stewards decided not to penalise him.

[[File:Kimi Canada 2008.jpg|thumb|right|Räikkönen driving for [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]] at the [[2008 Canadian Grand Prix]]]]
In [[2008 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]], Räikkönen qualified third. In the race, he set the fastest lap during the first stint while catching up with Robert Kubica who was in second place. The safety car was deployed when Adrian Sutil's car broke down in a dangerous position. Both he and Kubica jumped ahead of race leader Hamilton when they pitted during the safety car period. As there was a red light at the end of the pitlane, Räikkönen and Kubica stopped alongside each other and waited for the signal to allow them back onto the circuit. Hamilton failed to notice the red light and hit the rear of Räikkönen's Ferrari, eliminating both cars.

Räikkönen went on to take his 16th pole position in [[2008 French Grand Prix|France]], which was the 200th pole for Scuderia Ferrari.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/68489|title=Räikkönen on Ferrari's 200th pole – France|work=Autosport|date=21 June 2008|access-date=21 June 2008|archive-date=5 June 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090605031152/http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/68489|url-status=live}}</ref> Räikkönen dominated the race as he set the fastest lap and had a six-second lead until a bank exhaust failure some halfway through the race reduced his engine's power. He gave up the lead to his teammate Massa, but was far enough ahead of [[Toyota F1|Toyota's]] [[Jarno Trulli]], to secure second place and eight points.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/080622160456.shtml|title=Third win of the season for Massa|publisher=F1 Live|date=22 June 2008|access-date=23 June 2008|archive-date=24 June 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080624231131/http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/080622160456.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref>

Räikkönen qualified third at the {{F1 GP|2008|British}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/080705172836.shtml|title=A tough day for Raikkonen and Massa|publisher=F1 Live|date=5 July 2008|access-date=7 July 2008|archive-date=6 July 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080706225427/http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/080705172836.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref> Before the race, Räikkönen pushed noted photographer Paul-Henri Cahier to the ground as he lined up a close-up shot. Räikkönen's manager Steve Robertson claimed the driver was provoked by Cahier touching him with his lens and standing on his belongings, but Cahier disputed this version of events.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.itv-f1.com/news_article.aspx?id=43301 |title=Kimi branded 'arrogant' after grid fracas |publisher=ITV |date=8 July 2008 |access-date=1 September 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080825044136/http://www.itv-f1.com/news_article.aspx?id=43301 |archive-date=25 August 2008}}</ref><ref>Archived at [https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211211/S6e3bDs2lgw Ghostarchive]{{cbignore}} and the [https://web.archive.org/web/20130731012831/http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6e3bDs2lgw&feature=related Wayback Machine]{{cbignore}}: {{cite web |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S6e3bDs2lgw |title=Raikkonen loses his cool at Silverstone 2008|publisher=YouTube|date=7 July 2008 |access-date=1 September 2008}}{{cbignore}}</ref> The race was in wet conditions and Räikkönen stayed third at the first corner behind Hamilton and Kovalainen. He kept pace and got up to second when Kovalainen spun. He then chased after Hamilton, and set the fastest lap as he drew up directly behind the McLaren. During the first pitstop, Ferrari did not change the intermediates on his car in the hope that the track would become dry. However, the track was hit by another shower, and Räikkönen rapidly lost pace, and dropped down to sixth before finally pitting for new tyres. He finished fourth, a lap down.

At the {{F1 GP|2008|German}}, Räikkönen qualified sixth and dropped down a place at the first corner. He was running fifth when the safety car came out after a crash involving [[Timo Glock]]. His teammate Felipe Massa was ahead of him on the track, and as a result, Räikkönen was forced to wait behind Massa when the pitlane opened. This dropped him down to 12th, but he eventually finished in sixth.

At the {{F1 GP|2008|Hungarian}}, Räikkönen again qualified sixth. He lost a position to Alonso at the beginning of the race but managed to finish third owing to Hamilton's tyre puncture, passing Alonso during the pitstops and Massa's retirement after an engine failure.

[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2008 Belgium.jpg|thumb|right|Räikkönen at the [[2008 Belgian Grand Prix]], where he crashed on the penultimate lap after a duel with [[Lewis Hamilton]]]]
During the {{F1 GP|2008|European}}, Räikkönen qualified fourth and lost a place at the start to Kovalainen. He stayed fifth until the second round of pitstops when he exited before the fuel hose was properly disengaged from his car and left one of the mechanics with a fractured toe.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.gpupdate.net/en/f1-news/196700/ferrari-boss-defends-pit-stop-actions/|title=Ferrari boss defends pit stop actions|publisher=GPUpdate.net|date=25 August 2008|access-date=30 January 2011|archive-date=16 June 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120616061045/http://www.gpupdate.net/en/f1-news/196700/ferrari-boss-defends-pit-stop-actions/|url-status=live}}</ref> Two laps later, he suffered a similar engine failure to Massa in the previous race; a connecting rod in his engine broke and he was forced to retire.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.gpupdate.net/en/f1-news/196779/raikkonen-engine-problem-same-as-massa-s/|title=Raikkonen engine problem same as Massa's|publisher=GPUpdate.net|date=26 August 2008|access-date=30 January 2011|archive-date=16 June 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120616061056/http://www.gpupdate.net/en/f1-news/196779/raikkonen-engine-problem-same-as-massa-s/|url-status=live}}</ref>

At the {{F1 GP|2008|Belgian}}, Räikkönen again qualified fourth. He passed Kovalainen and Massa at the start to be second, and took the lead from Hamilton on the second lap. He pulled away, setting the fastest lap of the race and built a five-second gap. He looked set to win but owing to a late-race rain shower, Hamilton closed right up to him and tried to pass him at the final chicane with two laps to go. Hamilton cut the chicane and rejoined ahead of Räikkönen. He let Räikkönen take the place back. Hamilton then repassed him for the lead. The two battled on for the rest of the lap, with Räikkönen retaking the lead when the two stumbled upon spinning backmarker [[Nico Rosberg]], forcing Hamilton onto the grass. Räikkönen spun at the next corner and fell behind Hamilton again. While trying to catch up, he lost control of the car, smashed into a wall and retired.

At the {{F1 GP|2008|Italian}}, which was held in extremely wet conditions, Räikkönen qualified 14th. He stayed in 14th position for the first two stints. He climbed to ninth position in the third and last stint in which he also set the fastest lap of the race.

In [[2008 Singapore Grand Prix|Singapore]], the first night event in Formula One history, Räikkönen qualified third behind Massa and Hamilton. He remained in this position for most of the early laps. On lap 14, [[Nelson Piquet Jr.]]'s [[Renault in Formula One|Renault]] hit the wall at turn 17 and the safety car was deployed. Both Ferrari drivers pitted during the safety car period, with Räikkönen queued behind Massa in a busy pitlane. Ferrari released Massa before the fuel hose was disconnected from the car, which compromised Räikkönen who rejoined in 16th. Räikkönen managed to climb to fifth place, but on lap 57, while attacking [[Timo Glock]], he hit the wall after pushing too hard at turn 10 and retired.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/080928193627.shtml|title=Singapore disaster for Ferrari|publisher=F1live.com|date=28 September 2008|access-date=29 September 2008|archive-date=29 September 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080929084907/http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/080928193627.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.grandprix.com/race/r800raceincidents.html|title=Singapore GP – Sunday – Race Incidents|publisher=F1-Grandprix.com|date=28 September 2008|access-date=29 September 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080929163357/http://www.grandprix.com/race/r800raceincidents.html|archive-date=29 September 2008|url-status=dead}}</ref> He set the fastest lap of the race as his 10th of the season. This equalled Michael Schumacher's 2004 record of ten fastest laps in a Formula One season.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://f1pulse.com/drivers/Kimi_Raikkonen/3E403B/drivers_profile.aspx |title=Kimi Raikkonen: Profile |publisher=F1Pulse.com |access-date=22 September 2013 |archive-date=27 September 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130927222004/http://f1pulse.com/drivers/Kimi_Raikkonen/3E403B/drivers_profile.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref>

At the {{F1 GP|2008|Japanese}} at the [[Fuji Speedway]] circuit, Räikkönen qualified second on the grid, behind Hamilton, and took the lead at the start. Closing up to turn one, Hamilton attempted to pass on the inside, braked late and went wide,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/081012104703.shtml|title=Hamilton and Kovalainen left Fuji empty handed|publisher=F1live.com|date=12 October 2008|access-date=17 October 2008|archive-date=15 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081015022343/http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/081012104703.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref> forcing Räikkönen to also go wide.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/71342 |title=Alonso takes second win in a row at Fuji |work=Autosport |date=12 October 2008 |access-date=22 November 2008 |archive-date=9 November 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081109225740/http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/71342 |url-status=live }}</ref> Räikkönen lost out heavily and went down to seventh position. He gained places after a collision between Hamilton and Massa, Kovalainen's hydraulic failure and an overtaking manoeuvre on Jarno Trulli. He eventually finished third, behind Renault's Alonso and BMW Sauber's Robert Kubica.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/081012081723.shtml|title=Fantastic Fernando takes Fuji|publisher=F1live.com|date=12 October 2008|access-date=13 October 2008|archive-date=14 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081014153452/http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/081012081723.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref> This result meant that it was impossible for Räikkönen to retain his Drivers' Championship title for the second year.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/081013102335.shtml|title=Raikkonen drops out championship hunt|publisher=F1live.com|date=13 October 2008|access-date=14 October 2008|archive-date=14 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081014112834/http://en.f1-live.com/f1/en/headlines/news/detail/081013102335.shtml|url-status=live}}</ref>

In [[2008 Chinese Grand Prix|China]], Räikkönen qualified second behind Hamilton. At the start he stayed second with his teammate and now Ferrari's world championship contender, Massa, behind him in third place. However, with Räikkönen out of the running for the world championship he let Massa through into second place on lap 49, to help the latter gain two additional points in his pursuit of Hamilton in the world championship race.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12433_4356666,00.html|title=Kimi – After you Felipe|publisher=SkySports.com|date=19 October 2008|access-date=20 October 2008|archive-date=22 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081022031921/http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12433_4356666,00.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

At the {{F1 GP|2008|Brazilian}}, Räikkönen qualified third and finished third, behind Massa and Alonso. As Kubica failed to score, he finished third in the championship.

Räikkönen also won the [[DHL Fastest Lap Award]] for the second year in a row. He set 10 fastest laps throughout the season.

====2009====
[[File:Raikkonen test Ferrari F60.jpg|left|thumb|Räikkönen tests the [[Ferrari F60|F60]], Ferrari's 2009 challenger.]]
At the start of the {{F1|2009}} season in the {{F1 GP|2009|Australian}}, Räikkönen qualified in ninth place. The pace of the Ferraris and McLarens in particular was significantly slower than the likes of the [[Brawn GP|Brawn]], Red Bull and other outfits who were struggling to keep up with them in 2008. In the race, both Ferraris were running well before Räikkönen hit a barrier. He was forced to make an unscheduled pit stop on lap 43 and subsequently retired with differential failure.

[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2009 Turkey 3.jpg|right|thumb|Räikkönen at the [[2009 Turkish Grand Prix]]]]
In [[2009 Malaysian Grand Prix|Malaysia]], Räikkönen topped the time sheet in the second practice session.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7980532.stm|title=Raikkonen tops Malaysia practice|publisher=BBC|work=BBC Sport|date=3 April 2009|access-date=3 April 2009|archive-date=6 April 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090406034314/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/motorsport/formula_one/7980532.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> Räikkönen was ninth in qualifying. [[Sebastian Vettel]] and [[Rubens Barrichello]]'s ten and five-place penalties respectively meant that he was promoted to seventh. During the race, rain was predicted and the team took a gamble to change Räikkönen to full wet tyres while the track was still dry. The gamble did not pay off, and Räikkönen fell down the field. By the time the race was stopped on the 33rd lap due to torrential rain, Räikkönen was classified 14th.

Räikkönen's season did not get any better in Round 3 in [[2009 Chinese Grand Prix|China]] where he qualified in eighth place. In the wet race, he and Lewis Hamilton had duels early on, with Hamilton having to overtake Räikkönen three times to get the job done. Räikkönen complained about power loss from the engine from near the start and of a lack of grip after his one and only pit-stop. This meant that he could only finish 10th. In [[2009 Bahrain Grand Prix|Bahrain]], Räikkönen secured sixth place and Ferrari's first points of the year, but was disappointed by the team's performance. He retired from the {{F1 GP|2009|Spanish}} due to a hydraulics failure after qualifying from the back of the grid.

At the {{F1 GP|2009|Monaco}}, Räikkönen secured second place in qualifying, Ferrari and Räikkönen's best qualifying of the year so far. He admitted that he was still disappointed because he missed out on pole narrowly to the Brawn of [[Jenson Button]]. Räikkönen lost out to Rubens Barrichello at the start of the race, dropping back to third. He maintained this position until the chequered flag.

At the {{F1 GP|2009|Turkish}}, Räikkönen qualified sixth, but damaged his front wing on the first lap. He could only finish ninth, out of the points. At the {{F1 GP|2009|British}}, Räikkönen qualified ninth but a good start saw him move up to fifth. However, he dropped to eighth during the pit stops because of traffic and remained until the finish.

At the {{F1 GP|2009|German}}, Räikkönen qualified ninth after a damp session. In the race however he collided with the [[Force India]] of [[Adrian Sutil]] like in [[2008 Monaco Grand Prix|the previous year in Monte Carlo]], as the German was emerging from a pitstop. While Sutil managed to recover back to the pits to replace a nosecone, Räikkönen was forced to retire a few laps later with radiator damage as a result of the incident.

At the {{F1 GP|2009|Hungarian}}, Räikkönen took his and Ferrari's best finish of the season in second, after making a great start from seventh. After the first corner Räikkönen was in fourth place, but when Fernando Alonso retired after his early first stop, Räikkönen moved up to third. Räikkönen overtook Webber for second place at the first round of pit stops when Räikkönen and Webber pitted on the same lap. Räikkönen had a clean pit-stop, whereas Webber had a problem and was released into the path of the Ferrari. Räikkönen and Webber avoided collision, and Webber had to slot in behind Räikkönen. On his second pit stop, Räikkönen had a problem with an exhaust pipe. However, having built quite a gap between him and Webber, he held on to take second place.

[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2009 Italy 3.jpg|right|thumb|Räikkönen at the [[2009 Italian Grand Prix]]]]
At the {{F1 GP|2009|European}}, Räikkönen qualified sixth. He jumped to fourth at the start of the race. He then moved up to third after the second pit stops jumping [[Heikki Kovalainen]] for the last podium place, and stayed in that position until the end of the race, claiming his second straight podium.

At the {{F1 GP|2009|Belgian}}, Räikkönen qualified sixth, jumping to second at the start of the race. After the safety car was removed, he passed [[Giancarlo Fisichella]] to take the race lead and led all the way to the chequered flag for his first race win in 25 races, and the first and only one for Ferrari in 2009.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/78173 |title=F1 News: Raikkonen wins again as Fisichella stars |work=Autosport |date=30 August 2009 |access-date=13 September 2009 |archive-date=2 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090902084356/http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/78173 |url-status=live }}</ref> It was Räikkönen's fourth victory in the last five Belgian Grands Prix, bolstering his reputation as "The King of Spa".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.itv-f1.com/Feature.aspx?Type=General&id=46697 |title=Belgian GP factfile – Feature – F1 | ITV Sport |publisher=Itv-f1.com |date=26 August 2009 |access-date=13 September 2009 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090901034745/http://www.itv-f1.com/Feature.aspx?Type=General&id=46697 |archive-date= 1 September 2009}}</ref>

Räikkönen continued his good form at the {{F1 GP|2009|Italian}}, qualifying and finishing third, after Hamilton's last-lap crash. It was his fourth consecutive podium finish.

[[2009 Singapore Grand Prix|Singapore]] saw the end of a great run for Räikkönen where he only finished 10th after qualifying 12th.

In [[2009 Japanese Grand Prix|Japan]], Räikkönen came very close to another podium, finishing fourth. He had qualified fifth and was not able to gain a place at the start of the race, as he was on hard tyres. He put on softs for his second stint and was able to close in on [[Nick Heidfeld]] at about three-quarters of a second every lap. He overtook the German after the BMW Sauber came out of the pits. However, an accident involving [[Scuderia Toro Rosso|Toro Rosso]]'s [[Jaime Alguersuari]] brought out the safety car on lap 44, which kept the field stationary for a further five laps. Despite Hamilton suffering a KERS failure, Räikkönen's car did not have the grip necessary and was not able to overtake the third-placed McLaren at the restart. He went wide in an attempt to overtake Hamilton but recovered without losing a further place to [[Nico Rosberg]].

In [[2009 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]], Räikkönen qualified fifth and finished sixth. His race was already ruined when [[Mark Webber (racing driver)|Mark Webber]] swerved into his path, damaging the Ferrari's front wing. At the pit stop while having the wing changed, fuel dripping from the fuel rig stuck on Kovalainen's car caused the Ferrari to briefly burst in flames as the two cars were exiting their pit stops. For the rest of the race even with his eyes burning from fuel, Räikkönen used his strategy to move up the order and eventually finished in sixth place.

In [[2009 Abu Dhabi Grand Prix|Abu Dhabi]], the last race of the season, Räikkönen qualified 11th. He lost a place at the start of the race to [[Kamui Kobayashi]]. Räikkönen finished 12th, out of the points.

===First departure (2010–2011)===
Near the end of the 2009 season, Ferrari announced that Räikkönen would be leaving the team, despite having a contract to race for them in {{F1|2010}}. He would be replaced by Fernando Alonso.<ref name="move">{{cite news |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/8280566.stm |title=Ferrari confirm capture of Alonso |publisher=BBC Sport |date=30 September 2009 |access-date=30 September 2009 |archive-date=12 March 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120312190137/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/motorsport/formula_one/8280566.stm |url-status=live }}</ref> He was expected to return to McLaren alongside Lewis Hamilton but negotiations with the team failed.<ref>{{cite news |title=Raikkonen to take a sabbatical in 2010 |url=http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/80187 |first=Jonathan |last=Noble |work=autosport.com |publisher=[[Haymarket Group|Haymarket Publications]] |date=18 November 2009 |access-date=18 November 2009| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20091120135259/http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/80187| archive-date= 20 November 2009 | url-status= live}}</ref> Räikkönen was then linked to [[Mercedes-Benz in Formula One|Mercedes GP]] but the team eventually signed [[Michael Schumacher]] and [[Nico Rosberg]]. [[Toyota F1]], before it pulled out of Formula One, offered Räikkönen a driving contract to replace [[Timo Glock]] in 2010. The [[BBC]] reported that he refused the contract owing to wanting to drive a race-winning car, not to mention Toyota not offering a large enough salary.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/8394585.stm | publisher=BBC News | title=Raikkonen agrees rallying switch | date=4 December 2009 | access-date=7 May 2010 | archive-date=14 April 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230414023603/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/8394585.stm | url-status=live }}</ref>

On 17 November 2009, his manager Steve Robertson confirmed that Räikkönen would not drive in Formula One in the 2010 season.<ref>{{cite news | url=http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/80187 | title=Raikkonen to take a sabbatical in 2010 | work=Autosport | date=17 November 2009 | access-date=18 November 2009 | archive-date=20 November 2009 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091120135259/http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/80187 | url-status=live }}</ref> But during 2010 itself, rumours emerged once again about another possible Räikkönen comeback this time with the [[Renault in Formula One|Renault]] team in {{F1|2011}}. This followed a resurgence in Renault's form, and the fact that the Russian [[Vitaly Petrov]] had yet to be re-signed like team-mate [[Robert Kubica]]. Team principal [[Éric Boullier]] claimed he had been contacted by Räikkönen in connection with a possible return, but said that although he was flattered by Räikkönen's alleged display of interest:
{{blockquote|"I would have to speak personally with him first, look him in the eyes to see if I see enough motivation there for him to return to F1. It doesn't make sense to hire somebody, even a former world champion, if you cannot be sure that his motivation is still 100%. Why should you invest in somebody who leaves you guessing?"}}
However, Räikkönen angrily shot down the suggestion that he would race, claiming that Renault had simply used his name for "their own marketing purposes".<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.motorsport.com/news/article.asp?ID=390017&FS=F1 |title=Angry Raikkonen rules out F1 return with Renault |publisher=Motorsport.com |date=5 October 2010 |access-date=23 December 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101012180337/http://www.motorsport.com/news/article.asp?ID=390017&FS=F1 |archive-date=12 October 2010 |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite news | url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/9065307.stm | publisher=BBC News | title=Kimi Raikkonen rejects claims of F1 return at Renault | date=6 October 2010 | access-date=17 October 2010 | archive-date=14 April 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230414023603/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/motorsport/formula_one/9065307.stm | url-status=live }}</ref>

===Lotus (2012–2013)===
[[File:Kimi Raikkonen 2012 Malaysia Qualify.jpg|thumb|Räikkönen driving for Lotus at the [[2012 Malaysian Grand Prix]]]]
In the week before the [[2011 Singapore Grand Prix]], several news sources reported that Räikkönen was eyeing a return to Formula One for {{F1|2012}} with [[Williams Grand Prix Engineering|Williams]], after he was spotted at the team's headquarters in [[Grove, Oxfordshire]].<ref>{{cite news |last=Benson |first=Andrew |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/formula1/15024539 |title=Kimi Raikkonen chases F1 return with Williams in 2012 |publisher=BBC News |date=22 September 2011 |access-date=5 November 2011 |archive-date=25 October 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111025092833/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/formula_one/15024539.stm |url-status=live }}</ref> On 29 November 2011, it was announced that Räikkönen would be returning to Formula 1 in 2012, signing a two-year contract with [[Lotus F1|Lotus]].<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/formula1/15934329 | publisher=BBC News | title=Raikkonen to return to Formula 1 | date=29 November 2011 | access-date=29 November 2011 | archive-date=14 April 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230414023646/https://www.bbc.com/sport/formula1/15934329 | url-status=live }}</ref>

====2012====
Räikkönen began the season by qualifying 17th for the {{F1 GP|2012|Australian}} after making a mistake on his last flying lap. However, he recovered his weekend during the race the next day, as he made a good start to move up to twelfth, before making it into the top ten. He took three places on the last lap of the race, to finish in seventh place.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/98172|first=Simon|last=Strang|title=Raikkonen says qualifying mistakes cost him the chance of racing much closer to the front|date=18 March 2012|access-date=18 March 2012|work=[[Autosport]]|publisher=[[Haymarket Group|Haymarket Publications]]|archive-date=18 March 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120318154938/http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/98172|url-status=live}}</ref>

In the dry qualifying session for the {{F1 GP|2012|Malaysian}}, Räikkönen qualified in fifth place. He started the race from 10th place because of an unscheduled gearbox change. In the race he had the opportunity to try Pirelli's wet-weather tyres for the first time. He finished the race in fifth despite being inexperienced with Pirelli's wet-weather tyres compared to most of the other drivers.

At the {{F1 GP|2012|Chinese}}, Räikkönen was 14th after running second before being overtaken by 10 cars in the space of one lap, due to a mistake in tyre strategy, expecting the Pirelli tyres to last longer in the last stint. At the {{F1 GP|2012|Bahrain}} he finished second starting from 11th position on the grid. It was his first podium and his best finish of this comeback. Three weeks later, Räikkönen finished third at the {{F1 GP|2012|Spanish}}, after having qualified in fifth place, which had been promoted to fourth as a result of a penalty given to Lewis Hamilton.

In the {{F1 GP|2012|Monaco}}, Räikkönen qualified in eighth but finished one place lower in ninth place. For the {{F1 GP|2012|Canadian}} Räikkönen again had a bad qualifying session finishing 12th. He was able to finish eighth in the race taking, as he stated, "important championship points". In the {{F1 GP|2012|European}} at the [[Valencia Street Circuit]] he finished second behind Fernando Alonso.<ref>{{cite news|title=Alonso becomes first double winner in 2012 with superb European GP victory|url=http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/100680|work=Autosport|date=24 June 2012|access-date=24 June 2012|archive-date=14 July 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120714055813/http://www.autosport.com/news/report.php/id/100680|url-status=live}}</ref> In the {{F1 GP|2012|British}} he finished fifth, after having qualified in sixth place.

During qualifying for the {{F1 GP|2012|German}}, Räikkönen had good pace in Q1 with the harder set of tyres and was looking good for a potential shot at a higher grid place. At the start of Q2 the rain was already heavy meaning that Räikkönen lost the advantage he had in dry conditions. In the wet Q3 session he qualified in 10th place. Good pace in dry conditions from the qualifying session was proven in the race as Räikkönen succeeded to finish fourth, and eventually gaining the last podium spot after [[Sebastian Vettel]] was penalised for an illegal overtaking manoeuvre.

For the {{F1 GP|2012|Hungarian}}, Räikkönen qualified fifth. He lost fifth place at the start to Alonso due to a KERS issue on his E20 car. He succeeded in gaining a position over Alonso during the first round of pit-stops, and later did the same to Button and Vettel during the second round. He finished the race in second place, just one second behind the race winner Hamilton.

[[File:Kimi Räikkönen, United States Grand Prix, Austin 2012.jpg|thumb|Räikkönen at the [[2012 United States Grand Prix]]]]
After a five-week long summer break, the much speculated introduction of a 'Drag Reduction Device' by the Lotus F1 team was delayed due to bad weather conditions on Friday for the {{F1 GP|2012|Belgian}}. That did not stop Räikkönen from qualifying fourth. He went on to finish third behind [[Jenson Button]] and Sebastian Vettel. A week later at {{F1 GP|2012|Italian}} at Monza, Räikkönen qualified seventh and finished the race in fifth.

At the {{F1 GP|2012|Singapore}}, Räikkönen qualified 12th as his Lotus E20 could not match the pace of the top three teams and eventually finished sixth. At the {{F1 GP|2012|Japanese}}, Räikkönen qualified eighth after spinning at his final attempt in Q3 and finished sixth after being passed by McLaren's Lewis Hamilton after the second round of pit stops.

The {{F1 GP|2012|Korean}} saw the introduction of a coanda style exhaust system and several other minor updates to the Lotus E20 which, Lotus team boss Éric Boullier regarded as a new era<ref name="Lotus upgrades 'the opening of a new era'">{{cite news |url=http://www.espn.co.uk/lotusf1/motorsport/story/91227.html |title=Lotus upgrades 'the opening of a new era' |work=ESPN.co.uk |date=9 October 2012 |access-date=23 September 2012 |archive-date=27 September 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130927163716/http://www.espn.co.uk/lotusf1/motorsport/story/91227.html |url-status=live }}</ref> in his team's development race. Räikkönen eventually qualified fifth and finished the race in fifth after an impressive battle with Hamilton which ended in Räikkönen's favour.
